summary: Cantrill critiques Scott Rosenberg's 'Dreaming in Code' for framing software development primarily through the lens of difficulty rather than understanding software's unique nature as a confluence of information and machine. He argues that Rosenberg's choice to follow the doomed Chandler project led to an overly pessimistic view that misses software's most profound quality: its ability to achieve timeless perfection. Unlike physical structures that decay, software can become permanent infrastructure that future generations build upon, with each generation working at higher levels of abstraction.
key_points: Software is unique as a confluence of information and machine, not merely 'hard to build'
Rosenberg chose a doomed project (Chandler) which biased his conclusions about software development
Software can achieve absolute, timeless perfection unlike physical engineering
Successful software sediments into infrastructure, enabling future abstraction layers
To understand software development properly, one should study systems programmers building databases, operating systems, and VMs
money_quotes: If you insist on looking at the world through a bucket of shit, the world starts to look an awful lot like a bucket of shit.
Software – unlike everything else that we build – can achieve a timeless and absolute perfection.
Long after the Lighthouse at Alexandria crumbled, Euclid's greatest common divisor algorithm is showing no signs of wearing out.

Once software achieves something approximating perfection (and a surprising amount of it does), it sediments into the information infrastructure: the abstractions defined by the software become the bedrock that future generations may build upon.
PC-era zillionaire with too many ideas funds on-the-beach programmers to change the world with software that has no revenue model. Yikes – call me when you ship something…
key_insight: Software's true nature is not that it's merely difficult to build, but that it uniquely can achieve permanent perfection and sediment into foundational infrastructure that elevates all future work.
